title: Irreducible components of affine Deligne-Lusztig varieties and orbital integrals
speaker: Rong Zhou
abstract: Affine Deligne-Lusztig varieties (ADLV) naturally arise in the study of Shimura varieties and Rapoport-Zink spaces; their irreducible components give rise to interesting algebraic cycles on the special fiber of Shimura varieties. We prove a conjecture of Miaofen Chen and Xinwen Zhu, which relates the number of irreducible components of ADLV's to a certain weight multiplicity for a representation of the Langlands dual group.

proof_stages.label: Shimura-geometric source of the component problem
proof_stages.summary: The local factor x_mu(b) is isolated as the object encoding the geometry of basic loci in Shimura varieties, whose irreducible components give algebraic cycles and may occur with multiplicity.

proof_stages.label: Affine Deligne-Lusztig varieties and the Chen-Zhu conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines x_mu(b), the Kottwitz set B(G), Newton and Kottwitz invariants, the dimension d, the top-dimensional component set Sigma, the sigma-centralizer J_b, and states the conjectural formula |J_b(F)\Sigma| = dim V_mu(lambda_b).

proof_stages.label: Known cases, theorem, and reductions
proof_stages.summary: After recalling previous results, the speaker states the new proof for all triples and reduces the remaining argument to the case where G is simple adjoint, not of type A, with b basic and decent so that x_mu(b) is defined over a finite field.

proof_stages.label: Lang-Weil reduction to twisted orbital integrals
proof_stages.summary: A Lang-Weil-type asymptotic is adapted to the non-finite-type ADLV setting, expressing the leading term as a twisted orbital integral whose coefficients involve stabilizer volumes of top-dimensional components.

proof_stages.label: Base-change fundamental lemma conversion
proof_stages.summary: The base-change fundamental lemma converts the relevant twisted orbital integral over F_s into an ordinary orbital integral over F, which under the decency assumption becomes evaluation at the identity of the base-changed Hecke function.

proof_stages.label: Satake transform and leading weight multiplicity
proof_stages.summary: Using the Satake isomorphism, the base-change map is computed on the Weyl-invariant monomial basis, the Hecke function is compared asymptotically with the character chi_mu, and Kazhdan-Lusztig/Kostant partition estimates show that only the lambda_b^+ term contributes to the leading asymptotic.

proof_stages.label: Stabilizer volumes and the q=0 finish
proof_stages.summary: The stabilizers are shown to be parahorics whose volume contribution is governed by a rational function with constant term one; comparing with known cases identifies the remaining limit, and evaluating at q=0 makes each component orbit contribute one, giving the Chen-Zhu formula.

examples.name: Supersingular locus of a modular curve
examples.note: The modular curve case identifies the supersingular locus with a quaternionic class set and its local factor with a concrete GL2 affine Deligne-Lusztig set, making the later general definitions visible but not supplying the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker develops the modular-curve supersingular locus example concretely, writing the GL2 element b and cocharacter mu, identifying the affine Deligne-Lusztig set with Dieudonné modules/lattices and then with a quaternionic double quotient.

passes.reason: The supersingular locus is the basic/extreme Newton stratum of the modular curve, used as a motivating model rather than to refute a claim.
The supersingular locus on a modular curve is a finite zero-dimensional basic/Newton-extreme stratum used as a motivating model, not to refute a claim.
Zero-dimensional supersingular/basic Newton stratum of the modular curve, used only as motivation.
The supersingular locus is the basic/extreme Newton stratum in the modular-curve special fiber, used as a motivating model rather than to refute a claim.
The supersingular locus is a finite zero-dimensional basic Newton stratum, lying at the supersingular extreme for elliptic curves.
It is a finite zero-dimensional basic/supersingular Newton stratum, the extremal isoclinic endpoint for elliptic curves, used only as motivation.
the supersingular/basic Newton stratum on a modular curve is an extreme closed stratum and is zero-dimensional, used only as a motivating case.
The modular-curve supersingular locus is used as a motivating model, and structurally it is the zero-dimensional/basic extreme Newton stratum.
Used as a motivating case; the supersingular/basic locus is an extremal Newton stratum in the modular curve.
It is used as a motivating model, and structurally it is the finite zero-dimensional basic/supersingular Newton stratum of the modular curve.
It is used as a motivating model, and structurally it is the finite zero-dimensional basic/supersingular Newton stratum of the modular curve.
used as a motivating base case whose supersingular locus is a zero-dimensional finite set

examples.name: GL_n isocrystals and Newton polygons
examples.note: The speaker uses G=GL_n to explain that B(G) is the set of isocrystals and that the Newton point is the usual Newton polygon, illustrating the abstract invariants in the conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: GL_n isocrystals and Newton polygons are invoked as the standard interpretation of B(G) and the Newton point, but no particular GL_n isocrystal or Newton polygon is computed or checked.

passes.reason: GL_n isocrystals and their Newton polygons are the standard representative linear case for the general classification.
GL_n isocrystals classified by Newton polygons are the standard representative case of the general B(G) classification, with no sporadic or boundary structure invoked.
Standard GL_n isocrystal/Newton-polygon classification family, used as an illustrative comparison.
GL_n isocrystals classified by Newton polygons form the standard systematic case of the general theory.
GL_n isocrystals classified by Newton polygons form the standard systematic family rather than a special or limiting case.
GL_n isocrystals classified by arbitrary Newton polygons form the standard systematic case, not a special structural outlier.
GL_n isocrystals classified by Newton polygons are presented as the standard type-A model rather than a structurally special or refuting example.
GL_n isocrystals and Newton polygons are invoked as the standard representative case of the general classification, not as a special obstruction.
Used as the standard GL_n illustration of isocrystals classified by Newton polygons.
It is the standard type-A classification example for isocrystals via Newton polygons, with no special structural extremality or failure role singled out.
The GL_n isocrystal/Newton polygon case is cited as the standard representative classification example, not as a failure case or a special sporadic object.
used as the standard GL_n case where isocrystals are classified by ordinary Newton polygons

examples.name: Hilbert modular surface with inert p
examples.note: The Hilbert modular surface example has a positive-dimensional supersingular locus with two P1-fibration families over a quaternionic Shimura set, illustrating the nontrivial multiplicity predicted by the Chen-Zhu count.
examples.engagement_reason: The Hilbert modular surface with inert p is described as having a supersingular locus split into two P1-fibration families with multiplicity two, but this structure is stated rather than derived or worked through.

passes.reason: The inert-prime Hilbert modular surface is a standard example used to show that the multiplicity need not be one.
The Hilbert modular surface with inert p is presented as a defect-zero/basic-locus case, i.e. a minimal-defect extremal situation, illustrating multiplicity rather than contradicting anything.
Defect-zero Hilbert modular surface case with basic locus of multiplicity two, used to illustrate the phenomenon.

The inert-prime Hilbert modular surface is presented as a defect-zero/basic Shimura case, an extreme minimal-defect situation, illustrating nontrivial multiplicity rather than serving as a counterexample.
A Hilbert modular surface at an inert prime is a standard Shimura-variety example used to illustrate multiplicity two, not a structural exception.
The inert-prime Hilbert modular surface is used as a systematic defect-zero, hence minimal-defect, Shimura example with multiplicity two.
the inert Hilbert modular surface is a defect-zero boundary case used to show the multiplicity-one modular-curve picture can fail, with multiplicity two.
The inert-prime Hilbert modular surface is used to show the modular-curve multiplicity-one pattern fails, since its supersingular components occur with multiplicity two.
Used to illustrate nontrivial multiplicity in a standard Hilbert modular surface family, not as a sporadic or limiting object.
It is used to show the modular-curve multiplicity-one pattern need not persist, and the associated basic case has minimal defect zero.
It is used to illustrate a defect-zero case with multiplicity two, where defect zero is the minimal boundary value rather than a sporadic exception.
used as a defect-zero Hilbert modular example, i.e. a minimal-defect boundary case with multiplicity two
